generated_oml:
// C3: Physical sensing components
instance MotorPositionEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Position encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft for precise spatial measurement of robotic drive components"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorPositionDataTrace
]

instance GearboxPositionEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox providing additional positional feedback for drive units"
    DTDFVocab:producedData GearboxPositionDataTrace
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction data flows
instance MotorPositionDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorPositionEncoder
]

instance GearboxPositionDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom GearboxPositionEncoder
]

// C10: Data components
instance PositionalContextData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data traces from positional sensors (motor and gearbox encoders) for manufacturing monitoring purposes"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorPositionDataTrace, GearboxPositionDataTrace
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowProcessor
]

instance OperationalLogData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Structured operational logs and environmental parameters (temperature, pressure, motion data) from physical assets"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowProcessor
]

// C11: Enablers
instance DigitalShadowProcessor : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Software component implementing passive digital shadow architecture for one-way automated data flow from physical robotic systems"
    DTDFVocab:enables DataAggregationService, ContextualAnalysisService
]

instance SysMLModeler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Toolchain for SysML modeling (IBDs, state machines) and architecture description (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) for multi-layered system modeling"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RequirementsModel
]

instance SimulinkEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink environment for dynamic behavior modeling, state machine validation, and automata simulation"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo BehavioralSimulationModel
    DTDFVocab:enables DynamicAnalysisService
]

instance MBSEFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Model-Based Systems Engineering framework integrating RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) and OOSEM methodologies"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RequirementsModel, FunctionalDecompositionModel
    DTDFVocab:enables StakeholderAlignmentService
]

// C10: Models
instance RequirementsModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Comprehensive requirements documentation for manufacturing-based physical twin system using RFLP methodology"
]

instance FunctionalDecompositionModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Functional architecture decomposition following MBSE methodologies (OOSEM) for robotic control systems"
]

instance LogicalArchitectureModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Logical architecture representation using ADLs (ACME, AADL) for component-interconnection structures"
]

instance PhysicalTechnicalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Product/technical layer model describing physical robotic elements (motors, gearboxes) and Siemens SINUMERIK controllers"
]

instance BehavioralSimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "State machine and automata models (SysML v1.7, MontiArc) for dynamic behavior validation"
]

instance SimulationPipeline : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Simulation tool integration pipeline (Simulink + C++/Java) for dynamic analysis and validation"
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance PositionalAccuracyInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time positional accuracy assessment derived from encoder data for robotic drive units"
]

instance OperationalEfficiencyInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Performance metrics derived from contextual data traces for manufacturing process optimization"
]

instance MaintenanceAlertAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated maintenance scheduling triggered by anomaly detection in operational logs"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance ProcessOptimizationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Dynamic parameter adjustment recommendations based on behavioral simulation results"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// C6: Services
instance DataAggregationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service that collects and aggregates positional data traces from robotic encoders for digital shadow representation"
    DTDFVocab:provides PositionalAccuracyInsight
]

instance ContextualAnalysisService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service that processes contextual operational data for manufacturing monitoring and predictive analytics"
    DTDFVocab:provides OperationalEfficiencyInsight, MaintenanceAlertAction
]

instance DynamicAnalysisService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service that performs dynamic behavior validation through simulation pipelines (Simulink + C++)"
    DTDFVocab:provides SimulationResults
]

instance StakeholderAlignmentService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service that ensures consistency across heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives using MBSE methodologies"
    DTDFVocab:provides AlignmentDocument
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "
    The Digital Twin system in this context operates within a **real-time, data-driven** temporal framework, where synchronization between physical and digital components is governed by **contextual time intervals** for system properties. While explicit synchronization frequencies or latency requirements are not detailed, the system relies on **aggregated data traces** aggregated over specific time intervals to maintain alignment with physical processes. The **digital shadow** (a foundational component of the Digital Twin) is inherently passive, receiving one-way data flows from physical assets (e.g., manufacturing equipment like robot axes) without autonomous feedback. This implies a **low-latency, event-driven** approach where data aggregation and abstraction occur in near-real-time to support operational decision-making. The **MBSE (Model-Based Systems Engineering) methodology** used ensures that temporal dependencies are implicitly managed across hierarchical abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture), though no granular specifications (e.g., milliseconds/seconds for synchronization) are provided.
  "
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "A Digital Twin (DT) system in this context is structured around a **Digital Shadow Reference Model** that distinguishes between **digital shadows** (one-way, passive data flows from physical objects to digital representations) and **Digital Twins** (active components that interpret and process incoming data autonomously). The **Digital Shadow** is defined as a *‘set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose’* (Kritzinger et al. [32]). While the provided documents do not explicitly detail synchronization frequencies, temporal granularity, or latency requirements for twinning, the **hierarchical and modular nature of MBSE-based modeling** (e.g., SysML, ADLs like AADL or MontiArc) suggests that Digital Twin systems rely on **multi-level abstraction layers** (requirements → functional → logical → physical architecture) to manage temporal dependencies across heterogeneous stakeholders. The **active components** within Digital Twins (e.g., those enabling autonomous behavior) imply that time-sensitive operations are managed through **state machines or automata** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines) to ensure real-time synchronization between physical and digital states. However, no specific time scales, synchronization protocols, or granularity standards are explicitly mentioned in the documents."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
  A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the described context spans structured phases aligned with Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies, leveraging multi-abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Key stages include:
  - **Development Phase**: Utilizes SysML for architecture description (e.g., SysML IBDs, ACME, AADL, MontiArc) to model components, interfaces, and behavior via state machines or automata (e.g., MontiArc Automaton). Integration with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink or general-purpose languages (C++, Java) supports detailed behavior modeling.
  - **Digital Shadow Transition**: A Digital Shadow (DS) is introduced as a passive, one-way data flow entity (e.g., contextual data traces and aggregation) for specific purposes, transitioning from DS to DT via active components that interpret and process incoming data (e.g., software systems with embedded logic).
  - **Lifecycle Governance**: Implicitly governed by MBSE frameworks (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) that manage stakeholder perspectives and heterogeneous modeling notations/tools. Transition management between DS and DT phases relies on active data processing components to enable bidirectional interaction.
  The lifecycle emphasizes abstraction and integration across stakeholders, with tools like SysML and ADLs ensuring consistency across layers.
 "
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
  **Model Fidelity and Validation:**
  Fidelity is ensured through:
  - **Sensor arrangement** (e.g., dual-encoder setup) to minimize uncertainty in positional data.
  - **Active software components** interpret and process incoming sensor data, though no explicit error detection/correction mechanisms are described beyond data abstraction.
  **Validation/Verification:**
  The provided methodologies (**RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT**) are used to manage complexity across abstraction layers but do not explicitly detail validation/verification processes. Uncertainty quantification is implicitly addressed via sensor redundancy (e.g., dual encoders), though no explicit metrics (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations) or quality assurance processes are mentioned.
  **Quality Assurance:**
  The document does not specify formal validation/verification protocols or uncertainty quantification methods beyond sensor design. The Digital Shadow’s passive nature limits active error correction, relying instead on contextual data aggregation for abstraction.
  "
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The **digital shadow** relies on **MQTT** for data transmission, though no explicit communication protocols, network topology, bandwidth/latency requirements, or redundancy mechanisms are specified. The documents mention **SysML** for modeling but do not detail technical network infrastructure beyond the passive data flow context. Asset Administration Shells (AAS) are referenced for implementation but not as part of the technical connection layer."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The deployment infrastructure for the **digital shadow** is not explicitly detailed in the documents. The context only mentions **modeling tools** (e.g., Simulink, C++, Java) and a **dashboard** for data visualization, without specifying hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational/storage requirements, or scalability mechanisms. No explicit mention of databases, deployment models, or reliability assurances is provided."
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "
    The Digital Twin system integrates with external systems via **standardized protocols and interoperability frameworks**. Sensor data is streamed using **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport), enabling lightweight, event-based communication. The methodology emphasizes **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** for interoperability, ensuring compatibility with enterprise systems and Industry 4.0 standards. While no explicit dependencies on third-party systems are outlined, the **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** in MBSE (e.g., component & connector perspectives) implies modularity for external system coupling. The system’s architecture supports **data exchange protocols** (MQTT) and **abstraction layers** (AAS) to facilitate seamless interaction with both internal and external Digital Twin instances or enterprise IT ecosystems.
  "
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
Standardization in this Digital Twin system is governed by **specific Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**:
- **SysML** (System Modeling Language) for requirements and functional/logical architecture modeling.
- **AADL** (Aircraft Adapters and Architectures Language) for component-based system descriptions.
- **ACME** and **MontiArc** for component/connector perspectives and behavior modeling (e.g., automata).
No **ISO/IEC standards** or broader interoperability frameworks (e.g., IEC 62264) are mentioned. The document focuses on **tool-specific compliance** (e.g., Simulink integration) rather than industry-wide certifications or compliance frameworks.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system employs the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework for live-data linking and visualization, leveraging its structured ontology to ensure standardized asset representation and interoperability. While the AAS does not explicitly define security layers in the provided documents, it is implicitly assumed to support cybersecurity protocols through its integration with **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) for real-time data streaming, as referenced in Document 52 (though not explicitly named, the protocol is implied for IoT connectivity).

The system integrates **MATLAB Simulink** for model-driven engineering of digital twins in manufacturing (Document 43), where safety-critical systems are constrained by the **RAMI 4.0** reference architecture (Document 48), which defines layers for safety and security alignment. The physical robot (e.g., MABI robotic arm, Document 44) operates within a safety cell whose working range is restricted to predefined limits, ensuring collision avoidance and fail-safe mechanisms without explicit emergency stop details. The system’s safety considerations are grounded in **friction modeling** (Document 45) for structured learning of robot dynamics, ensuring predictable and controlled motion within operational boundaries.

No explicit compliance with data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR) or privacy policies is documented, and no mention of consent management, anonymization, or sharing agreements is provided."
]
